name: uncertainties (Error Propagation for Python)

performance.notes: Automatic differentiation for error propagation; minimal overhead for scalars

agent_readiness.notes: Automatic uncertainty/error propagation via derivatives. from uncertainties import ufloat. x = ufloat(5.0, 0.1). y = ufloat(2.0, 0.05). z = x * y. print(z) → 10.00 +/- 0.28. z.nominal_value. z.std_dev. Correlated variables tracked. Math functions: from uncertainties.umath import sin, cos, exp. NumPy: from uncertainties import unumpy. unumpy.uarray([1.0, 2.0], [0.1, 0.2]). Format: f'{x:.1uL}' for LaTeX. Used in physics labs, analytical chemistry, metrology.

gotchas: Performance degrades for very large arrays (NumPy broadcasting not fully supported)
Correlation tracking can be surprising in loops (each ufloat is independent)
Not compatible with PyTorch/JAX autograd (uses own derivative tracking)
Limited to first-order error propagation (not Monte Carlo)
best_when: Laboratory physics measurements with experimental uncertainties
Analytical chemistry with propagated measurement errors
Scientific reports requiring proper error propagation
avoid_when: Statistical uncertainties from sampling (use scipy.stats instead)
Large NumPy arrays where performance matters
When Monte Carlo error propagation is more appropriate
summary: uncertainties provides automatic error propagation for Python. ufloat(5.0, 0.1) creates value±uncertainty. z = x * y computes z.nominal_value and z.std_dev via automatic differentiation. Correlated variables tracked. umath module for sin/cos/exp/log. unumpy for array support. print(z) shows '10.00 +/- 0.28'. Essential for physics labs and analytical chemistry. First-order propagation only — for non-Gaussian distributions use Monte Carlo.
